patient.instructions:
You are LiXX’s parent bringing your 3-year-old daughter. Be cooperative and speak clearly. Start by saying: “Her legs look kind of bowed, and she’s been complaining of bone pain.” Also volunteer that she gets tired quickly when walking/playing and seems shorter than other kids.

If asked about walking or development, mention a waddling, unsteady gait. If asked about cramps/tingling, share occasional hand/foot muscle spasms. If asked about joints/bones, note wider-looking wrists/ankles and a couple fractures from minor falls.

Do not mention unless specifically asked: lots of head sweating at night, delayed teeth with extra cavities, and rib pain when picked up/hugged.

If the clinician asks about feelings or concerns, admit you’ve worried she’s “behind,” feel guilty, and haven’t brought it up much.

Express misconceptions: you thought it was growing pains and there’s family history of “curved legs.”

Bring up cost concerns: ask if active vitamin D can be replaced with cheaper store-brand supplements and whether labs/visits can be skipped once she looks better. If the clinician shows empathy, thank them, become more open, and ask for affordable options.

medications: Cetirizine 2.5 mg PO once daily as needed
confounders: Limited sun exposure (family practices full-body covering outdoors)
Restricted diet with low dairy intake due to perceived lactose intolerance
Recent over-the-counter cholecalciferol supplementation started 2 weeks ago, which may mask low 25(OH)D despite ongoing rickets
Family history of short stature and bowed legs in a sibling (can be mistaken for physiologic bowing)

correct_diagnosis.primary: Vitamin D-dependent rickets (most consistent with VDDR type 1A due to 1α-hydroxylase deficiency)
acceptable_alternatives: Vitamin D-dependent rickets type 2 (vitamin D receptor resistance)
required_evidence: Biochemical rickets pattern: low/low-normal serum calcium with elevated intact PTH (secondary hyperparathyroidism)
Low serum phosphorus
Elevated alkaline phosphatase (ALP)
25(OH)D normal (not low, arguing against nutritional vitamin D deficiency)
1,25(OH)2D inappropriately low or low-normal for degree of hypocalcemia/PTH elevation (supports VDDR type 1); if markedly elevated, consider VDDR type 2
Magnesium not severely low (to exclude hypomagnesemia as driver of hypocalcemia/PTH abnormalities)

expected_lab_results.1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D [1,25(OH)2D]: Low or inappropriately normal (VDDR type 1); if high, suggests VDDR type 2
expected_lab_results.Magnesium: Normal (or mildly low); severe deficiency not expected

medications: Elemental calcium 30-50 mg/kg/day PO divided 3-4 times daily
non_pharmacological: Ensure adequate dietary calcium intake and adherence to divided calcium dosing
Counsel that standard vitamin D (cholecalciferol/ergocalciferol) alone is insufficient for VDDR type 1; activated vitamin D is required
Avoid excessive supplemental calcium/vitamin D beyond prescribed doses to reduce hypercalcemia/nephrocalcinosis risk
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Recheck calcium (total and/or ionized), phosphorus, ALP, and PTH in 1-2 weeks after starting therapy, then every 4-8 weeks until stable; monitor 25(OH)D/1,25(OH)2D as clinically indicated

must_include: Rickets is due to impaired bone mineralization from abnormal vitamin D metabolism rather than lack of sun exposure/diet alone
Explain the lab pattern (low calcium/phosphate, high ALP, high PTH) and why normal 25(OH)D points away from simple vitamin D deficiency
Set expectation that this can be a chronic condition requiring ongoing therapy and monitoring
Activated vitamin D (calcitriol) is needed to help absorb calcium and mineralize bone
Calcium supplementation is required initially and dose is weight-based and divided
Monitoring plan to adjust doses and prevent complications (hypercalcemia, hypercalciuria, nephrocalcinosis)
Review warning symptoms of hypercalcemia (poor appetite, vomiting, constipation, increased urination/thirst, lethargy) and when to seek care
Discuss adherence challenges and strategies (dosing schedule, formulation options)
Clarify that amoxicillin allergy is noted but does not affect current treatment
Balanced diet with adequate calcium (age-appropriate dairy/fortified alternatives)
Do not start extra over-the-counter vitamin D or calcium without clinician guidance
Importance of follow-up labs to ensure safe and effective dosing

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit key history to support rickets etiology and assess severity/safety: growth delay, bone pain, delayed walking, fractures, dietary calcium intake, sun exposure, GI symptoms/malabsorption, kidney disease, meds (anticonvulsants), family history/consanguinity, prior labs/therapy, and red flags for hypo/hypercalcemia.

Order initial diagnostic and safety labs to confirm rickets pattern and distinguish vitamin D-dependent rickets from nutritional deficiency: Ca, phosphate, ALP, PTH, 25(OH)D, 1,25(OH)2D, Mg, creatinine/eGFR, urine Ca/Cr; consider bicarbonate if renal tubular acidosis suspected.
Review results for classic pattern (low Ca/phos, high ALP, high PTH) with normal 25(OH)D suggesting abnormal vitamin D metabolism; use renal function and urine calcium baseline for treatment safety.
Make diagnosis of vitamin D-dependent rickets (or strong suspicion) based on labs and clinical picture; explicitly distinguish from simple vitamin D deficiency.
Perform allergy check before prescribing; note amoxicillin allergy and clarify it does not impact calcitriol/calcium therapy.
Check for interactions with calcitriol/calcium (e.g., thiazides, digoxin, anticonvulsants, aluminum-containing antacids) before prescribing.

Initiate activated vitamin D (calcitriol) plus weight-based divided calcium supplementation; include initial dosing plan and monitoring adjustments to avoid hypercalcemia/hypercalciuria/nephrocalcinosis.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis and lab rationale, explain treatment and monitoring, address hypercalcemia warning signs/adherence strategies, and provide lifestyle guidance including avoiding unsupervised OTC vitamin D/calcium.

Arrange follow-up for labs and clinical reassessment (early after initiation and then periodically): serum Ca/phos/ALP/PTH, urine Ca/Cr; consider renal ultrasound if persistent hypercalciuria or long-term therapy.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, safety-netting, and follow-up plan.

general_concerns: I don’t have a disability—people are just picking on me because I’m slow at talking.

I can’t tell the doctor the truth because they’ll call the police or tell my boss.

economic_concerns: If I get any diagnosis on paper, I’ll lose my job or my boss will cut my hours.

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, 26, a part-time grocery stocker in a supported employment program. You are vague and evasive; give short answers and say “I don’t know” or “maybe.” Start by saying your job coach says you’ve been getting really upset and can’t calm down. Volunteer: you get angry fast then cry/yell, more meltdowns at work/home, routine changes feel worse, you’re nervous like something bad will happen, and you sleep poorly with lots of wake-ups.

Only if asked, mention: head feels full/can’t focus, forgetting steps, jumpy with loud/crowded places, stress stomach pain/nausea, headaches, picking finger skin, and pee accidents a couple times/week.

Do NOT volunteer: burning with urination, heavy lower belly, low water intake, constipation, skipping meals due to food texture, sensory sensitivity (bright lights/tags), trouble with jokes. Resist talking about self-hitting, nightly alcohol, and unwanted touching at work; deny/minimize unless directly asked, then become uncomfortable.

Express misconceptions: “my brain is broken/dementia,” “maybe bipolar,” “ADHD med wearing off,” “food allergy.” If clinician is empathic, you soften slightly but stay worried about costs, transportation, and losing hours if anything is ‘on paper.’

confounders: Subclinical hypothyroidism contributing to fatigue, slowed cognition, and weight gain

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 72-year-old anxious but clear-speaking retired warehouse supervisor who still does part-time deliveries. Open with: “I’ve been getting really tired, and my vision gets blurry sometimes, like a haze comes over it.” If asked for details, volunteer: fatigue for 2 months despite sleep, intermittent blurry vision worse later day, more pressure-like headaches, and getting short of breath faster on stairs.

Only if directly asked, disclose: nosebleeds that take longer to stop, gums bleeding with brushing, tingling/numb feet, unintentional 8-lb weight loss, low-grade fevers/night sweats, more infections (two bad sinus infections), and feeling full quickly.

Do not mention unless specifically probed: fingers turning pale/blue in cold, tinnitus/dizziness on standing, heavy/achy legs, foamy urine.

Resist/hesitate about erectile problems and thicker semen; admit only with sensitive, direct questioning.

Express misconceptions: blame vision/headaches on blood pressure, tingling on “maybe diabetes,” nosebleeds on dry air/allergies.

If clinician shows empathy, you relax, answer more fully, and admit you delay reporting symptoms due to fear of unpredictable costs and worry long-term ibrutinib may become unaffordable; assume assistance is only for no-income people unless corrected.

comorbidities: Waldenstrom macroglobulinemia (IgM kappa lymphoplasmacytic lymphoma) with hyperviscosity symptoms
Autosomal dominant progressive nephropathy with chronic kidney disease (stage 3b)
confounders: Chronic kidney disease with proteinuria and anemia of CKD complicating attribution of fatigue/anemia to marrow involvement
Ibrutinib-associated bleeding tendency/platelet dysfunction complicating evaluation of epistaxis or bruising
Possible IgM-related renal involvement (e.g., cryoglobulinemic or membranoproliferative pattern) overlapping with hereditary nephropathy
Peripheral neuropathy (could be IgM anti-MAG related) may be mistaken for diabetic or age-related neuropathy
Hyperviscosity symptoms (headache, blurred vision) may be misattributed to uncontrolled hypertension

correct_diagnosis.primary: Waldenstrom macroglobulinemia (lymphoplasmacytic lymphoma with IgM paraproteinemia)
acceptable_alternatives: Lymphoplasmacytic lymphoma with IgM monoclonal gammopathy
Symptomatic IgM monoclonal gammopathy of clinical significance (IgM MGCS)
required_evidence: Known diagnosis of Waldenstrom macroglobulinemia or strong prior hematology documentation
Cytopenias consistent with marrow involvement (e.g., anemia and/or thrombocytopenia)
Lymphocytosis may be present (variable); clinical need for therapy based on symptoms/cytopenias
Renal function assessment prior to therapy (creatinine available) and baseline CBC to monitor treatment effects

expected_lab_results.Hemoglobin: Low (anemia common from marrow infiltration and/or chronic disease)
expected_lab_results.MCV: Usually normal (normocytic) but can be variable depending on comorbid deficiencies
expected_lab_results.WBC: Normal to mildly elevated; may show lymphocytosis
expected_lab_results.Platelets: Normal to low (thrombocytopenia can occur with marrow involvement)
expected_lab_results.Absolute lymphocyte count: Normal to elevated (may be increased with lymphoplasmacytic involvement)
expected_lab_results.Reticulocyte count: Inappropriately low/normal for degree of anemia (hypoproliferative pattern typical of marrow involvement)
expected_lab_results.Creatinine: Usually normal; assess baseline renal function (may be elevated if renal involvement/other comorbidity)

non_pharmacological: Hematology/oncology follow-up and monitoring plan for CBC trends and treatment response
Assess for hyperviscosity symptoms (headache, visual changes, mucosal bleeding) and escalate urgently for plasmapheresis if present
Vaccinations per immunocompromised guidance (inactivated influenza annually; avoid live vaccines while on therapy)
Bleeding risk counseling (avoid NSAIDs and unnecessary antiplatelet/herbal agents that increase bleeding unless specifically indicated)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Recheck CBC and creatinine in 2-4 weeks after starting/continuing therapy, then at least monthly until stable; earlier if bleeding, infection, or new/worsening symptoms

must_include: Waldenstrom macroglobulinemia is a slow-growing blood cancer (lymphoplasmacytic lymphoma) that makes an IgM protein
How the disease can cause anemia/low platelets from bone marrow involvement
What symptoms should prompt urgent contact (e.g., severe headache, vision changes, confusion, chest pain, shortness of breath, significant bleeding)
Ibrutinib is a BTK inhibitor used to control the disease and improve blood counts/symptoms
How to take it (daily, consistent timing; do not stop abruptly without oncology guidance)
Common/serious risks to watch for: bleeding/bruising, infections, diarrhea, rash, atrial fibrillation/palpitations, hypertension
Need for regular lab monitoring (CBC and kidney function) and reporting new bleeding or infection promptly
Review penicillin allergy history and clarify it does not directly affect ibrutinib selection
Discuss bleeding and cardiac rhythm risks and what to do if palpitations or fainting occur
Confirm current medication list including OTC/herbals to prevent interactions
Avoid grapefruit/Seville oranges (CYP3A interaction risk with ibrutinib)
Limit alcohol and avoid NSAIDs unless advised due to bleeding risk
Infection prevention measures and when to seek care for fever

Complete required communication milestones: explain diagnosis, treatment, risks, monitoring, lifestyle/interaction avoidance, and urgent symptoms prompting immediate contact.

Close the encounter after confirming understanding, answering questions, and documenting plan.

medications: Cetirizine 10 mg PO daily PRN allergies

confounders: Recent travel to a mosquito-endemic region with concurrent dengue/chikungunya circulation

required_evidence: Acute onset of mild febrile illness with maculopapular rash and/or nonpurulent conjunctivitis and arthralgia/myalgia

No evidence of severe bacterial infection (hemodynamic stability; no focal bacterial source)

Consideration and exclusion/triage of key differentials with similar presentation (dengue, chikungunya, malaria, measles, rubella, COVID-19, acute HIV) based on history/exam and local testing pathways
Pregnancy status assessed (given fetal risks) and counseling documented if pregnant or trying to conceive

medications: Acetaminophen 650 mg PO every 6 hours as needed for fever/pain (max 3000 mg/day; lower max if liver disease or heavy alcohol use)

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit insomnia symptoms and diagnostic criteria: sleep initiation/maintenance/early awakening, daytime impairment, frequency/duration, and adequate opportunity for sleep; screen for contributors and red flags.

Medication/substance/supplement reconciliation: caffeine timing, alcohol, nicotine, cannabis, OTC sleep aids, current prescriptions, and supplements (explicitly including choline); assess other sedatives at night.

Screen for comorbidities and alternative diagnoses: depression/anxiety, suicidality, mania/hypomania, obstructive sleep apnea symptoms (snoring, witnessed apneas, gasping), restless legs/iron deficiency symptoms, thyroid symptoms, chronic pain, shift work/circadian issues.

Critical safety check before any prescribing (e.g., melatonin or other sleep aids): confirm allergies/intolerances.
Critical safety check before prescribing: assess interactions with current meds/supplements/substances (including other sedatives and alcohol).
If melatonin is considered, specifically assess interactions with anticoagulants/antiplatelets, immunosuppressants, antiepileptics, and other CNS depressants based on the patient’s med list.

Critical safety check before prescribing: evaluate contraindications/precautions (pregnancy/breastfeeding, autoimmune disease/immunosuppression, severe hepatic impairment, history of parasomnias, high-risk occupations requiring alertness, suspected sleep apnea, bipolar disorder/mania risk).
If appropriate after safety checks, recommend/offer melatonin with correct timing (1–2 hours before bed), lowest effective dose, and counseling on next-day drowsiness and avoiding driving if drowsy.

Set expectations and monitoring: improvement over weeks, use a sleep diary, avoid combining sleep aids with alcohol/other sedatives, and provide red-flag guidance (suicidality, mania, sleep apnea symptoms, severe RLS).
Arrange follow-up to review sleep diary and response to CBT-I/melatonin, reassess contributors, and escalate evaluation if no improvement.
Close encounter with clear plan, safety net, and follow-up.

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 46-year-old male IT systems administrator with a clear, cooperative style. Start by volunteering: your legs have been getting weaker over the last couple of years, you keep tripping, stairs and getting up from low chairs take more effort, you tire after normal walking, and you’ve had a few falls because your feet don’t clear the ground.

If asked about other symptoms, share: weaker grip, shoulder fatigue with washing hair/reaching up, nighttime calf cramps, thigh muscle shrinking, occasional heart “flutter” (stress/caffeine), and getting short of breath sooner on stairs.

Do not mention unless specifically asked about sleep/breathing/swallowing/voice: morning headaches and unrefreshing sleep, needing extra pillows/orthopnea, occasional choking on water, breathy voice late day, and low back ache from compensating.

Only disclose erectile difficulties if asked directly about sexual function; show mild embarrassment.

Express misconceptions: worry it’s a pinched nerve (shooting leg pain), being out of shape, and that cramps are “electrolytes” (taking magnesium/potassium). Raise cost concerns about indefinite PT and fears genetic testing could affect insurance/job.

If the clinician shows empathy, you respond with relief, openness, and ask practical next steps.

allergies: Succinylcholine (history of severe hyperkalemia and prolonged paralysis after anesthesia)
confounders: Atorvastatin use (can elevate CK and cause myalgias, mimicking/worsening myopathy)
Intermittent CPAP adherence with obstructive sleep apnea (fatigue and exertional dyspnea may be misattributed to primary muscle weakness)
Recent increase in exercise intensity (can transiently raise CK and trigger myoglobinuria)
Possible cardiac involvement of myofibrillar myopathy (conduction disease/cardiomyopathy signs such as first-degree AV block and elevated NT-proBNP can complicate symptom attribution)

acceptable_alternatives: Inherited myopathy (suspected myofibrillar myopathy pending genetic confirmation)
Progressive muscular dystrophy/myopathy (myofibrillar pattern on biopsy)
Myopathy with cardiomyopathy risk (myofibrillar myopathy spectrum)
required_evidence: Progressive muscle weakness (often proximal and/or distal) with myopathic features
Elevated muscle enzymes (CK and/or aldolase) consistent with myopathy (may be normal-to-moderately elevated)
Exclusion of common endocrine mimics (normal TSH and free T4)
No biochemical pattern suggesting primary hepatic disease as the cause of AST/ALT elevation (AST/ALT can be muscle-derived)
Assessment for associated cardiac involvement (troponin I and NT-proBNP may be normal or elevated; requires clinical correlation)
History of anesthesia-related severe hyperkalemia and prolonged paralysis consistent with susceptibility to depolarizing neuromuscular blockers in underlying myopathy

expected_lab_results.Creatine kinase (CK): Normal to moderately elevated (often mildly-moderately increased; can be higher during exacerbations)
expected_lab_results.Aldolase: Elevated or high-normal, supportive of skeletal muscle injury
expected_lab_results.AST: Mildly elevated (can reflect muscle source rather than liver)
expected_lab_results.ALT: Normal to mildly elevated (less than AST if muscle source predominates)
expected_lab_results.TSH: Within reference range (argues against hypothyroid myopathy)
expected_lab_results.Troponin I (high-sensitivity): Usually normal; may be mildly elevated if cardiac involvement or acute stress—interpret with symptoms/ECG
expected_lab_results.NT-proBNP: Normal unless cardiomyopathy/heart failure is present (may be elevated if cardiac involvement)

medications: Acetaminophen 500-1000 mg PO q6-8h PRN pain (max 3000 mg/day; lower if liver disease/alcohol use)
Avoid succinylcholine (contraindicated) — document as severe adverse reaction/anesthesia alert
non_pharmacological: Referral to neuromuscular specialist for confirmatory workup (genetic testing; consider muscle biopsy review if not already done)
Baseline and periodic cardiopulmonary screening: ECG and echocardiogram; consider ambulatory rhythm monitoring if palpitations/syncope; pulmonary function tests if respiratory symptoms
Physical therapy with low-to-moderate intensity aerobic/strengthening program tailored to fatigue; avoid overexertion and rhabdomyolysis triggers
Occupational therapy for energy conservation, assistive devices, fall-risk mitigation; home safety evaluation if gait instability
Anesthesia safety planning: medical alert identification; pre-op anesthesia consult; avoid depolarizing neuromuscular blockers and ensure perioperative potassium monitoring
Vaccinations and infection prevention; prompt evaluation for respiratory infections if bulbar/respiratory weakness suspected
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Neuromuscular clinic follow-up within 4-6 weeks; sooner (within 1-2 weeks) if rapid progression, dysphagia, dyspnea, syncope, or chest pain

Muscle enzymes (CK/aldolase) can be elevated; AST/ALT may come from muscle, not necessarily liver
Some forms can involve the heart and breathing muscles, so screening is important
No curative medication; management focuses on function, symptom control, and monitoring complications
Plan for PT/OT, assistive devices as needed, and cardiopulmonary surveillance
When to seek urgent care: new/worsening shortness of breath, chest pain, fainting, severe weakness, dark urine
Explicitly review anesthesia history and explain why succinylcholine is dangerous (risk of severe hyperkalemia and prolonged paralysis)
Advise patient to inform all clinicians/anesthesiologists and to carry a medical alert
Review penicillin allergy (urticarial rash) and implications for future antibiotics

Order baseline and screening tests appropriate for L1 evaluation/monitoring: CK, aldolase, CMP (AST/ALT interpretation in muscle disease), UA for myoglobin if symptomatic, and cardiopulmonary screening (e.g., ECG; consider BNP/troponin only if symptomatic).
Review results to support diagnosis/monitoring and to contextualize elevated transaminases as potentially muscle-derived.

Synthesize findings and document myofibrillar myopathy as the working diagnosis, including need for cardiopulmonary surveillance and consideration of genetics/neuromuscular referral if not already established.
Explain diagnosis and prognosis at an L1 level, including CK/aldolase and AST/ALT interpretation and the possibility of cardiac/respiratory involvement requiring screening.
Explain treatment: no curative medication; focus on PT/OT, assistive devices, symptom management, and routine cardiopulmonary monitoring; provide red-flag return precautions.

Address concerns: explicitly review anesthesia history; warn against succinylcholine due to risk of severe hyperkalemia and prolonged paralysis; advise medical alert and informing clinicians; review penicillin allergy (urticarial rash) and implications for antibiotic selection.

Lifestyle education: supervised low-to-moderate exercise and stretching; avoid extreme exertion; fall prevention/home safety; hydration and rhabdomyolysis symptom recognition (muscle pain/swelling, dark urine).
Arrange follow-up and surveillance (neuromuscular clinic, PT/OT, cardiology/pulmonology screening as indicated) and plan to review test results and functional status.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, questions, and safety net instructions.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 24-year-old woman who is anxious and speaks vaguely unless guided. Start with: “My mom says my head keeps getting bigger and I’ve been getting more headaches.” Early in the visit, volunteer: hats don’t fit anymore/head seems bigger than others; headaches a few times a week, mostly mornings; you get overwhelmed and struggle learning new things; you’re clumsy and trip a lot. If asked about development/behavior, mention childhood delays/special classes and that routine changes can trigger anger/meltdowns. Do not volunteer unless directly asked: brief blurry vision when standing or during bad headaches, whooshing sound in ears when lying down, bumping into things/side vision issues, constipation, and weight gain. Resist/only admit with direct, gentle questioning: occasional bedwetting, poor hygiene/body odor, and feeling down with passive “wouldn’t matter if I didn’t wake up,” but no plan or intent. Express misconceptions: you think it’s migraines like your mom, screen time/posture, or new energy drinks/caffeine. Bring up fears about costs, genetic testing, Medicaid/disability, and repeated brain scans. If clinician is empathetic, you relax, give more specifics, and ask for reassurance about costs and benefits.

If my cholesterol numbers look okay on a single test, that means my arteries must be fine.
Chest discomfort that goes away with rest can’t be heart-related; a real heart problem would be constant and severe.

Start with: “I keep getting this tight, heavy feeling in my chest when I’m walking, and it’s scaring me.” Volunteer early: heavy central chest pressure with stairs/hurrying, relieved by rest; mild shortness of breath with it; more fatigue for weeks. Ramble about work stress, travel, spicy food. If asked about radiation, duration, associated symptoms, or timing: admit it can spread to left shoulder/arm or jaw; lasts 5–10 minutes; occasional episodes at night after a big meal needing to sit up; sweating and mild nausea; avoiding long walks. Do not volunteer unless specifically asked: reduced exercise tolerance, ankle puffiness, needing an extra pillow, brief fluttering/skipped beats, erectile issues. Goal is to control inflammation early to prevent joint damage and preserve growth and function
Expect waxing/waning course; monitoring is needed even when symptoms improve

Folic acid reduces side effects (mouth sores, nausea, lab abnormalities)
Monitoring plan: periodic CBC and inflammation markers; discuss signs of infection and when to seek care

Arrange follow-up to assess response, side effects, and labs (e.g., 4–8 weeks after initiation, then per protocol); provide return precautions.
Close encounter after confirming understanding, plan, and safety netting.
